nodes.name: Countries of the World
nodes.description: Country sets by region and category

config.prompt: Name all 48 sovereign countries of Asia

items.explanation:
Landlocked country in Central/South Asia; capital is Kabul
Bordered by Pakistan, Iran, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China
Site of prolonged conflict — Soviet invasion (1979–1989), Taliban rule (1996–2001, 2021–present), U.S./NATO operations (2001–2021)
The Khyber Pass, one of the most famous mountain passes in history, connects Afghanistan to Pakistan

Landlocked country in the South Caucasus; capital is Yerevan
First country to adopt Christianity as a state religion (301 CE)
The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923) killed an estimated 1.5 million Armenians under the Ottoman Empire
Mount Ararat, the traditional resting place of Noah's Ark, is visible from Yerevan but lies across the border in Turkey

Located in the South Caucasus on the Caspian Sea; capital is Baku
Known as the 'Land of Fire' due to natural gas vents that have burned for millennia
Baku sits on massive oil reserves; the city had its first oil boom in the 1870s, predating most Middle Eastern oil development
Fought two wars with Armenia over the Nagorno-Karabakh region (1988–1994, 2020)

Island nation in the Persian Gulf off the coast of Saudi Arabia; capital is Manama
Smallest Arab country by area (~780 km²); connected to Saudi Arabia by the King Fahd Causeway (25 km)
Site of ancient Dilmun civilization, a major trading hub between Mesopotamia and the Indus Valley (~3000 BCE)
First Gulf state to discover oil (1932), but reserves are now largely depleted; economy shifted to banking and finance

Located in South Asia on the Bay of Bengal, almost entirely surrounded by India; capital is Dhaka
One of the most densely populated countries in the world (~170 million people in an area smaller than Iowa)
Gained independence from Pakistan in 1971 after a brutal war that killed an estimated 300,000–3 million people
The Ganges–Brahmaputra Delta, the world's largest river delta, makes the country extremely vulnerable to flooding and cyclones

Landlocked Himalayan kingdom between China and India; capital is Thimphu
Measures national success by Gross National Happiness rather than GDP
Last country in the world to introduce television (1999); banned tobacco sales entirely
The only carbon-negative country in the world — forests cover over 70% of its territory

Small sultanate on the island of Borneo, bordered by Malaysia; capital is Bandar Seri Begawan
One of the wealthiest countries per capita due to oil and gas reserves; citizens pay no income tax
The Sultan of Brunei is one of the world's richest monarchs; the Istana Nurul Iman palace has 1,788 rooms — the largest residential palace in the world
Once controlled most of Borneo and parts of the Philippines (15th–17th centuries)

Located in mainland Southeast Asia; capital is Phnom Penh
Home to Angkor Wat, the world's largest religious monument, built in the 12th century by the Khmer Empire
The Khmer Rouge regime under Pol Pot (1975–1979) killed an estimated 1.5–2 million people — roughly a quarter of the population
The Tonlé Sap lake reverses its flow seasonally, expanding to six times its dry-season size during monsoons

Most populous country in the world until 2023 (surpassed by India); capital is Beijing
The Great Wall stretches over 21,000 km; the country invented paper, printing, gunpowder, and the compass
World's second-largest economy by nominal GDP and largest by purchasing power parity
Chinese civilization is one of the oldest continuous civilizations, with written records dating back over 3,000 years

Island nation in the eastern Mediterranean; capital is Nicosia
Divided since 1974 when Turkey invaded the north — split between the Republic of Cyprus (EU member) and the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus (recognized only by Turkey)
Nicosia is the last divided capital city in the world
In Greek mythology, the birthplace of Aphrodite, goddess of love

Located in the South Caucasus at the crossroads of Europe and Asia; capital is Tbilisi
One of the oldest winemaking regions in the world — archaeological evidence of wine production dates to ~6000 BCE
Birthplace of Joseph Stalin (born in Gori, 1878)
Russia recognized and militarily supports two breakaway regions: Abkhazia and South Ossetia (since the 2008 war)

Located in South Asia; capital is New Delhi; most populous country in the world as of 2023
The world's largest democracy by population; gained independence from Britain in 1947 under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi
Birthplace of Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism
Home to the Taj Mahal (built 1632–1653), one of the New Seven Wonders of the World

World's largest archipelago (~17,000 islands) in Southeast Asia; capital is Jakarta (moving to Nusantara on Borneo)
Fourth most populous country in the world (~275 million) and the most populous Muslim-majority country
Home to Komodo dragons (the world's largest lizards) and Krakatoa, whose 1883 eruption was one of the deadliest in recorded history
The island of Java is the most populous island in the world (~150 million people)

Located in Western Asia; capital is Tehran
Successor state to the Persian Empire, one of the largest empires in ancient history (Achaemenid Empire under Cyrus the Great, ~550–330 BCE)
The 1979 Islamic Revolution overthrew Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and established a theocratic republic under Ayatollah Khomeini
Iran–Iraq War (1980–1988) was one of the longest conventional wars of the 20th century, killing an estimated 500,000–1 million

Located in the Middle East between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers; capital is Baghdad
Ancient Mesopotamia, often called the 'Cradle of Civilization' — earliest known writing (cuneiform), agriculture, and cities emerged here
Baghdad was the world's largest city and intellectual capital during the Islamic Golden Age (8th–14th centuries)
Invaded by U.S.-led coalition in 2003 on the basis of alleged weapons of mass destruction, which were never found

Located on the eastern Mediterranean coast; capital claimed as Jerusalem (disputed; most embassies are in Tel Aviv)
Established in 1948 as a Jewish state; the Israeli–Palestinian conflict has defined the region's geopolitics since
The Dead Sea, on its border with Jordan, is the lowest point on Earth's surface (~430 m below sea level)
Leads the world in per-capita tech startups and R&D spending; compulsory military service for most citizens

Island nation in East Asia comprising ~6,800 islands; capital is Tokyo
World's third-largest economy by nominal GDP; pioneered post-WWII economic recovery known as the 'Japanese economic miracle'
Only country to have been attacked with nuclear weapons (Hiroshima and Nagasaki, August 1945)
Has the world's oldest continuous monarchy — the imperial line claims descent from 660 BCE

Located in the Middle East, bordered by Israel, Saudi Arabia, Iraq, and Syria; capital is Amman
Home to Petra, a Nabataean city carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs (~300 BCE), one of the New Seven Wonders of the World
One of the most water-scarce countries in the world; hosts one of the largest refugee populations per capita (Syrian, Palestinian, Iraqi)
The Jordan River, forming its western border, is one of the most historically significant rivers in Christianity and Judaism

Largest landlocked country in the world (~2.7 million km²); capital is Astana (renamed from Nur-Sultan in 2022)
Spans Central Asia from the Caspian Sea to China; the ninth-largest country in the world by area
Home to the Baikonur Cosmodrome, the world's first and largest space launch facility — Yuri Gagarin launched from here in 1961
The Kazakh steppe is believed to be the region where horses were first domesticated (~3500 BCE)

Small country at the northern tip of the Persian Gulf; capital is Kuwait City
Iraqi invasion of Kuwait in August 1990 triggered the Gulf War (1990–1991); liberated by a U.S.-led coalition in Operation Desert Storm
One of the wealthiest countries per capita due to enormous oil reserves (~6% of world reserves)
One of the hottest countries on Earth — temperatures regularly exceed 50°C (122°F) in summer

Mountainous, landlocked country in Central Asia; capital is Bishkek
Over 90% of the country is above 1,500 m elevation; the Tian Shan mountain range dominates its landscape
Issyk-Kul is the world's second-largest alpine lake (after Titicaca) and never freezes despite being surrounded by mountains
The national epic poem, the Epic of Manas, is one of the longest in the world — roughly 20 times longer than the Iliad and Odyssey combined

Landlocked country in mainland Southeast Asia; capital is Vientiane
The most heavily bombed country per capita in history — the U.S. dropped over 2 million tons of ordnance during the Secret War (1964–1973)
Unexploded ordnance still contaminates roughly one-third of the country's land area
One of the last remaining communist states; the Mekong River forms most of its border with Thailand

Small country on the eastern Mediterranean coast; capital is Beirut
Ancient Phoenicia was centered in modern Lebanon — the Phoenicians invented the alphabet that became the basis for Greek, Latin, and most modern scripts
Civil war (1975–1990) devastated the country; Beirut was once called the 'Paris of the Middle East'
The Cedars of Lebanon, referenced extensively in the Bible, appear on the national flag

Located in Southeast Asia, split between the Malay Peninsula and northern Borneo; capital is Kuala Lumpur
The Petronas Towers in Kuala Lumpur were the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004 (452 m)
Multiethnic society of Malay, Chinese, and Indian populations; the Malay language uses a Latin alphabet adapted from the earlier Jawi (Arabic) script
World's largest producer of palm oil, a major driver of tropical deforestation

Island nation of ~1,200 coral islands in the Indian Ocean southwest of India; capital is Malé
Lowest-lying country in the world — average elevation is just 1.5 m above sea level; existentially threatened by rising sea levels
Smallest Asian country by both area (~300 km²) and population (~500,000)
Became the first country to hold a cabinet meeting underwater (2009) to highlight climate change

Landlocked country between Russia and China; capital is Ulaanbaatar
The Mongol Empire under Genghis Khan (13th century) was the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching from Korea to Eastern Europe
Most sparsely populated sovereign country in the world (~2 people per km²)
Ulaanbaatar is the coldest capital city in the world, with winter temperatures regularly reaching −30°C (−22°F)

Located in mainland Southeast Asia on the Bay of Bengal; capital is Naypyidaw (moved from Yangon in 2006)
Formerly known as Burma; renamed by the military government in 1989; both names remain in common use
Military coup in February 2021 overthrew the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi, who had won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1991
Home to Shwedagon Pagoda in Yangon, one of the oldest and most sacred Buddhist sites in the world

Landlocked Himalayan country between China and India; capital is Kathmandu
Home to eight of the world's fourteen peaks over 8,000 m, including Mount Everest (8,849 m) on the China–Nepal border
The only country in the world whose flag is not rectangular — it consists of two stacked triangles
Birthplace of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) in Lumbini (~563 BCE)

Located on the northern half of the Korean Peninsula; capital is Pyongyang
One of the most isolated and secretive states in the world; ruled by the Kim dynasty since 1948 (Kim Il-sung, Kim Jong-il, Kim Jong-un)
The Korean War (1950–1953) ended in armistice, not a peace treaty — the two Koreas are technically still at war
The DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) between North and South Korea is the most heavily fortified border in the world

Located on the southeastern Arabian Peninsula; capital is Muscat
Controls the Strait of Hormuz (with Iran), through which roughly 20–30% of the world's oil passes
The Omani Empire was a major maritime power in the 18th–19th centuries, controlling territories from Zanzibar to Pakistan
One of the oldest continuously inhabited regions in the Arabian Peninsula; frankincense from Oman's Dhofar region was prized in the ancient world

Located in South Asia; capital is Islamabad; largest city is Karachi
Created in 1947 by the Partition of India as a homeland for South Asian Muslims; originally included East Pakistan (now Bangladesh)
Sixth most populous country in the world (~230 million); the only Muslim-majority country with nuclear weapons
Home to the Indus Valley Civilization (Mohenjo-daro, Harappa), one of the world's earliest urban civilizations (~2500 BCE)

Archipelago of over 7,600 islands in Southeast Asia; capital is Manila
Named after King Philip II of Spain; was a Spanish colony for over 300 years (1565–1898), then a U.S. territory until 1946
The only predominantly Christian country in Asia (~90% Christian, mostly Catholic)
Situated in the Pacific Ring of Fire and the typhoon belt — one of the most disaster-prone countries in the world

Small peninsula jutting into the Persian Gulf from Saudi Arabia; capital is Doha
Highest GDP per capita in the world (by some measures) due to enormous natural gas reserves — home to the North Field, the world's largest gas field
Hosted the 2022 FIFA World Cup, the first held in the Middle East and the first held in November–December
Home to Al Jazeera, one of the most influential Arabic-language news networks, founded in 1996

World's largest country by area (~17.1 million km²), spanning Eastern Europe and northern Asia; capital is Moscow
Transcontinental state — roughly 77% of its territory is in Asia, though the majority of its population lives west of the Urals
Former core of the Soviet Union (1922–1991), the Cold War superpower; world's largest nuclear arsenal
Lake Baikal in Siberia is the deepest lake in the world (1,642 m) and holds about 20% of the world's unfrozen surface freshwater

Largest country in the Middle East, occupying most of the Arabian Peninsula; capital is Riyadh
Guardian of Islam's two holiest cities: Mecca (birthplace of Muhammad, site of the Kaaba) and Medina
World's largest oil exporter and home to the state oil company Saudi Aramco, the most profitable company in the world
Named after the House of Saud, the ruling family that unified the kingdom in 1932

City-state and island country at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula; capital is Singapore
One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita and a major global financial center, despite having almost no natural resources
Expelled from Malaysia in 1965; transformed from a developing country to a first-world economy in one generation under Lee Kuan Yew
One of the most densely populated countries in the world (~8,000 people per km²); famous for strict laws (chewing gum sales banned since 1992)

Located on the southern half of the Korean Peninsula; capital is Seoul
Transformed from one of the poorest countries in the world in the 1950s to the 10th-largest economy — the 'Miracle on the Han River'
Global cultural powerhouse: K-pop, Korean cinema (Parasite won Best Picture at the 2020 Oscars), and Korean cuisine have achieved worldwide reach
Samsung, Hyundai, and LG are among its major chaebols (family-controlled conglomerates)

Island nation in the Indian Ocean off the southeastern coast of India; capital is Sri Jayawardenepura Kotte (commercial capital: Colombo)
Formerly known as Ceylon; major producer of tea (Ceylon tea), cinnamon, and gemstones (especially sapphires)
Civil war between the Sinhalese-majority government and Tamil Tiger separatists lasted 26 years (1983–2009)
One of the highest literacy rates in South Asia (~92%); Buddhism is the majority religion, with a history spanning over 2,000 years on the island

Located in the Middle East on the eastern Mediterranean; capital is Damascus
Damascus is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, with evidence of settlement dating to ~10,000 BCE
Civil war since 2011 has killed over 500,000 people and displaced roughly half the pre-war population
Ancient Syria was a crossroads of empires — Assyrian, Persian, Greek, Roman, and Ottoman rule all left their mark; Palmyra was a major Greco-Roman city

Mountainous, landlocked country in Central Asia; capital is Dushanbe
Over 90% of the country is mountainous; the Pamir Mountains in eastern Tajikistan are known as the 'Roof of the World'
Poorest of the former Soviet republics; suffered a civil war (1992–1997) shortly after independence
The Tajik people are ethnically and linguistically Persian, distinct from the Turkic populations of neighboring Central Asian states

Located in mainland Southeast Asia; capital is Bangkok (Thai name: Krung Thep Maha Nakhon)
The only Southeast Asian country never colonized by a European power; formerly known as Siam until 1939
The Thai monarchy is protected by strict lèse-majesté laws — criticizing the king can carry a prison sentence of up to 15 years
Bangkok's full ceremonial name in Thai is the longest place name in the world (168 characters)

Located on the eastern half of the island of Timor in Southeast Asia; capital is Dili
One of the newest countries in the world — gained independence from Indonesia in 2002 after a 1999 referendum
Portuguese colony from the 16th century until 1975; Indonesia then invaded and occupied it for 24 years, during which an estimated 100,000–180,000 people died
One of the poorest countries in Asia; economy heavily dependent on oil and gas from the Timor Sea

Transcontinental country spanning southeastern Europe and western Asia; capital is Ankara; largest city is Istanbul
Istanbul (formerly Constantinople/Byzantium) straddles the Bosphorus strait and was capital of the Byzantine and Ottoman Empires
The Ottoman Empire (1299–1922) was one of the longest-lasting empires in history; modern Turkey was founded by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk in 1923
Hagia Sophia, built in 537 CE as a cathedral, is one of the greatest architectural achievements in history — converted to a mosque, then a museum, then back to a mosque (2020)

Landlocked country in Central Asia; capital is Ashgabat
Home to the Darvaza gas crater ('Door to Hell'), a natural gas field that has been burning since 1971
One of the world's most authoritarian states; former president Saparmurat Niyazov renamed months after himself and his family, banned opera and ballet, and required a golden statue of himself to rotate to face the sun
Over 70% of the country is covered by the Karakum Desert; holds the world's fourth-largest natural gas reserves

Federation of seven emirates on the Arabian Peninsula; capital is Abu Dhabi; largest city is Dubai
Dubai is home to the Burj Khalifa (828 m), the world's tallest building since 2010
Transformed from a pearling and fishing economy to a global commercial hub in roughly 50 years
Each emirate (Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al Quwain, Ras Al Khaimah, Fujairah) retains significant autonomy; Abu Dhabi holds over 90% of the UAE's oil reserves

Doubly landlocked country in Central Asia (one of only two in the world); capital is Tashkent
Home to Samarkand and Bukhara, two of the most important cities on the ancient Silk Road, with stunning Islamic architecture
The Aral Sea, once the fourth-largest lake in the world, has nearly disappeared due to Soviet-era irrigation diversions — one of the worst environmental disasters in history
Most populous country in Central Asia (~35 million)

Located in mainland Southeast Asia along the South China Sea; capital is Hanoi; largest city is Ho Chi Minh City (formerly Saigon)
The Vietnam War (1955–1975) killed an estimated 2–3 million Vietnamese and ~58,000 Americans; ended with the fall of Saigon
One of the fastest-growing economies in Asia since the Đổi Mới (economic renovation) reforms of 1986
Hạ Long Bay, with its ~1,600 limestone karst islands, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most recognizable landscapes in Southeast Asia

Located on the southern tip of the Arabian Peninsula; capital is Sana'a (contested; Aden serves as temporary capital)
The ancient Kingdom of Sheba (Saba) was centered in modern Yemen; Romans called the region 'Arabia Felix' (Fortunate Arabia) for its fertility
Civil war since 2014 between Houthi rebels and the internationally recognized government has created one of the world's worst humanitarian crises
Sana'a's Old City, with its distinctive multi-story rammed-earth tower houses, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world
